word_list.word: happy is the country which has no history
word_list.pronunciation: hˈæpi ɪz ðə kˈʌntɹi wˌɪtʃ hɐz nˈoʊ hˈɪstɚɹi

senses.definition: used to imply that a nation that has experienced little conflict or turmoil in its past is likely to be more stable, peaceful, and prosperous in the present and future

examples.x: Some people believe that happy is the country which has no history, and seek to preserve their country's peaceful state by avoiding conflict and confrontation.

senses.definition: used to imply unconditional loyalty and support for one's country, regardless of its actions or policies

word_list.word: a language is a dialect with an army and (a|) navy
word_list.pronunciation: ɐ lˈæŋɡwɪdʒ ɪz ɐ dˈaɪəlɛkt wɪð ɐn ˈɑːɹmi ænd ɐ nˈeɪvi

senses.definition: used to imply that the distinction between a language and a dialect is often determined by political power and influence rather than inherent linguistic differences

examples.x: While all forms of speech are valuable and deserve respect, some varieties are associated with more power and prestige - a language is a dialect with an army and navy, so be aware of the political factors that influence our perceptions of language.

word_list.word: we are all Adam's children, but silk makes (all|) the difference
word_list.pronunciation: wiː ɑːɹ ˈɔːl ˈædəmz tʃˈɪldɹən bˌʌt sˈɪlk mˌeɪks ˈɔːl ðə dˈɪfɹəns

senses.definition: used to imply that all human beings are equal in their fundamental nature or origin, but social status or external factors like wealth and privilege can create significant distinctions or disparities among them

word_list.word: a cat (may|can) look at a king
word_list.pronunciation: ɐ kˈæt mˈeɪ kæn lˈʊk æɾə kˈɪŋ

senses.definition: used to imply that everyone has fundamental rights and freedoms, regardless of their social status or position, and that no one is above the law or immune to scrutiny

examples.x: Although he was just a lowly clerk, he refused to be intimidated by his boss's authority - a cat may look at a king, and he had every right to question and challenge his supervisor's decisions.

senses.definition: used to imply that in many situations, those who have more power or resources can exploit or dominate those who have less

examples.x: In the cutthroat world of business, it's often said that big fish eat little fish, and that the most successful companies are those that can outcompete and dominate their rivals.

word_list.word: disobedience is the true foundation of liberty
word_list.pronunciation: dˌɪsoʊbˈiːdiəns ɪz ðə tɹˈuː faʊndˈeɪʃən ʌv lˈɪbɚɾi

senses.definition: used to emphasize the need for individuals or groups to stand up against oppressive systems, structures, or regimes in order to secure and promote freedom

examples.x: The protests against the authoritarian regime were a manifestation of the belief that disobedience is the true foundation of liberty, as citizens stood up against oppression to demand greater freedom.

senses.definition: used to suggest that those who have nothing to lose, such as beggars, may be more fearless and willing to take risks, including participating in rebellion or uprising

examples.x: Whether it's in politics, social movements, or any other area of life, those who are the most vulnerable or marginalized may also be the most fearless and willing to fight for their rights - beggars fear no rebellion, as the old saying goes.

word_list.word: it takes all sorts to make a world
word_list.pronunciation: ɪt tˈeɪks ˈɔːl sˈɔːɹts tə mˌeɪk ɐ wˈɜːld

senses.definition: used to imply that diversity and individual differences among people are necessary for the world to function and thrive

examples.x: He’s eccentric, but it takes all sorts to make a world.
People have different views on success—after all, it takes all sorts to make a world.
It’s strange, but it takes all sorts to make a world, and that’s what makes life interesting.
Not everyone likes the same food, but it takes all sorts to make a world.
She might be quiet, but remember, it takes all sorts to make a world.

word_list.word: a great city, a great solitude
word_list.pronunciation: ɐ ɡɹˈeɪt sˈɪɾi ɐ ɡɹˈeɪt sˈɑːlɪtˌuːd

senses.definition: used to imply that urban life can lead to feelings of loneliness and isolation due to the difficulty of forming meaningful social connections
